title: Wei Li · LTRG — Method Distillation
lede: Method-usage tricks of the Linearized Tensor Renormalization Group (LTRG) and its closest successor, the Exponential Tensor Renormalization Group (XTRG), as practiced by Wei Li — distilled from the originating LTRG paper (Li et al., PRL 2010) and the XTRG paper (Chen, Chen, Chen, Li & Weichselbaum, PRX 2018).

sections.title: Scope, tooling & cost
sections.note: Corpus: the originating LTRG paper and its closest successor XTRG. Cases, tooling, and cost are grounded in these two; the later tanTRG variant is named only as a beyond-corpus survey point.

rows: ==Finite-temperature thermodynamics== — free energy, internal energy, specific heat — of one- and two-dimensional quantum lattice models, reaching the ==thermodynamic limit== (an effectively infinite system) in one dimension; XTRG extends the reach to two dimensions and to ==exponentially low temperature==.

One-dimensional spin-½ XY and Heisenberg chains (both papers; XTRG down to inverse temperature $\beta \approx 82$ in units of $1/J$); two-dimensional ==square-lattice Heisenberg== ($4\times4$ versus exact diagonalization, $16\times5$ versus quantum Monte Carlo) and ==frustrated triangular-lattice Heisenberg== width-4 cylinders cooled to $T/J \approx 0.03$ (XTRG). Beyond corpus: the later tanTRG variant reaches the two-dimensional Hubbard model.

Two-dimensional quantum magnets at finite temperature for which quantum Monte Carlo is obstructed by the ==sign problem== (negative sampling weights), and systems with ==well-separated energy scales== where reaching the lowest scale cheaply is decisive.

In two dimensions the accessible ==cylinder width is set by the bond dimension==, not the site count: XTRG validates width-4 triangular and width-5 square lattices quantitatively, while width-8 appears only in its cost estimate. Validation is restricted to where exact-diagonalization or quantum-Monte-Carlo reference data exist.

XTRG's non-Abelian symmetry is built on the ==QSpace tensor library==; LTRG and its successors are otherwise ==implemented in-house== (the authors' QMagen package, MATLAB). Controlling parameters are intrinsic to the method — the bond dimension $D$ (number of retained states, or $D^*$ multiplets under non-Abelian symmetry) and the imaginary-time / initialization step $\tau$.

A one-dimensional core is ==a few hundred lines== on a tensor / singular-value-decomposition library; the principal difficulties are ==maintaining a stable truncation toward low temperature== and implementing the symmetry-resolved (non-Abelian) tensor contractions. Line counts are not stated in the papers and remain an interview target.

Per-run time complexity (XTRG'18 Tab. I): XTRG scales as $O(\ln\,\beta\, N D^4)$ against LTRG's $O((\beta/\tau)\, N D^3)$, $N$ = number of sites — so XTRG needs ==only logarithmically many cooling steps==. XTRG is reported faster than the series-expansion variant by ==more than one order of magnitude==; non-Abelian SU(2) symmetry adds an $O((D/D^*)^m)$ gain with $D/D^* \approx 3$–$4$. ==Absolute core-hours are not reported== and remain an interview target.

sections.title: Method-usage tricks
sections.note: Techniques for executing the method reliably. LTRG'10 supplies the linearized-evolution craft; XTRG'18 the exponential-cooling craft.

rows: The transfer-matrix network is decimated ==one row per imaginary-time step==, discarding only the smallest singular values per step — versus the older coarse-graining renormalization (TRG), which discards substantially more.
Origin of LTRG's ==accuracy and scalability== over TRG.

Singular values are rescaled by their largest element at each step to ==prevent divergence== of the imaginary-time evolution; the free energy is reconstructed from the accumulated normalization factors.
==The discarded norms encode the free energy==.

Independent variation of the imaginary-time step and the bond dimension isolates the error source: at ==high temperature== it saturates in bond dimension (time-discretization–limited); at ==low temperature== it decreases with bond dimension (truncation-limited).
Identifies ==which parameter limits accuracy== in each regime.
Pairwise contraction of $2^p$ matrices over $p$ steps yields $2^p$ sites — here $2^{100}$, ==effectively the thermodynamic limit== — at a cost growing only with $p$.
==Removes finite-size error== at negligible cost in one dimension.

Each step squares the density operator, $\rho_n = \rho_{n-1}^2$, so the inverse temperature doubles, $\beta \to 2\beta$, per step. The result is ==exponentially fast cooling with far fewer truncation steps== than a linear imaginary-time grid.
Reaches low temperature ==exponentially fast==, and the ==fewer truncations also raise accuracy==.

The starting operator $\rho(\tau_0)$ at exponentially small $\tau_0$ is built from a ==Maclaurin/Taylor series== of $e^{-\tau_0 H}$ — down to the lowest-order form $1-\tau_0 H$ — using no Trotter–Suzuki gates, so arbitrary one- and two-dimensional long-range Hamiltonians initialize cleanly.
==Removes the Trotter error at its root== and sidesteps the gate construction that is cumbersome for 2D Hamiltonians.
**Non-Abelian SU(2) symmetry in the thermal MPO**

The density operator is represented in a ==multiplet basis==, so each bond carries $D^*$ multiplets rather than $D$ states ($D/D^* \approx 3$–$4$ for spin-½), cutting cost by $O((D/D^*)^m)$. This is the ==first such implementation for finite-temperature MPOs==.
Order-of-magnitude saving that ==makes 2D clusters reachable== at high precision.

The thermal state is formed as $\rho(\beta) = \rho(\tau)^\dagger \rho(\tau)$ by contracting the half-$\beta$ operator with its conjugate, which keeps $\rho(\beta)$ ==positive semidefinite even after truncation== and yields the partition function as the squared Frobenius norm $Z = \Vert\rho(\tau)\Vert_F^2$ — a ==free extra factor-of-two in cooling==.
Numerically robust thermal state plus ==cheaper access to the free energy==.

A Trotter–Suzuki decomposition recasts the $D$-dimensional quantum model as a ==classical tensor network in one higher dimension==; the thermal density operator becomes a matrix product operator (a one-dimensional tensor chain).
The internal energy is read from a ==local impurity tensor== inserted in the network, cross-validated against the temperature derivative of the free energy.
A measurement intrinsic to the network, ==not finite differencing==.

Because the MPO entanglement of a thermal state grows only as $S_E \approx 3c\,\ln\,\beta$ ($c$ = central charge), the inverse temperature must change on a ==relative, not absolute, scale==; a logarithmic $\beta$ grid is therefore the natural discretization, and it bounds the bond dimension to grow only ==algebraically (critical) or stay constant (gapped)== rather than as the feared $e^\beta$.
Tells you ==both the grid and why low temperature is tractable== at all.

Doubling jumps directly to the temperature of a ==low-lying feature== a linear grid cannot reach affordably — for example the Kitaev gauge-flux specific-heat peak at $T_l/T_h \sim 10^{-2}$, or the triangular-lattice two-peak structure ($T_l$ and $T_h$).
Exposes ==physics hidden between widely separated energy scales==.

sections.title: Open problems & improvement directions
sections.note: Gaps are lifted from the corpus itself — its statements or its own data — never inferred. A gap that the expert's own later paper in the corpus resolves is shown as a one-line trajectory; only still-open problems enter the table. The directions that would close them are interview targets, not filled here.

blocks.label: Closed within the corpus —
blocks.text: ==High-temperature Trotter–Suzuki floor.== LTRG'10 (Fig. 4) flags that the relative error stops improving with bond dimension for $T>0.2J$ and yields only to a smaller imaginary-time step. XTRG'18 removes it structurally: the method is Trotter-free — series-expansion initialization at $\tau_0$ as small as $10^{-6}$ (controlled to $<10^{-15}$) followed by exact $\rho \to \rho^2$ doubling. ==Flagged in LTRG'10 → closed in XTRG'18==, so it is no longer an open problem.

rows: Across LTRG and XTRG, fermions appear only as the ==free-fermion XY chain== (mapped to a tight-binding model); XTRG's outlook lists "interacting fermionic systems" as a target but shows none. Interacting fermions such as the ==Hubbard model== are exactly the high-value ==sign-problem== cases the family is pitched at.

The specific heat from numerical differentiation of the free energy on the logarithmic grid develops =="minor wiggles" at the lowest temperatures==, compounded by MPO truncation at large $\beta$; XTRG'18 ==shades the lowest-temperature regions of Fig. 7(c,f) as unreliable==.

blocks.title: Interview targets (not settled in the corpus)
items: Improvement directions — how to push past each open problem above — for the expert to supply.

Did the later tanTRG variant (beyond this corpus) actually deliver interacting-fermion, two-dimensional Hubbard thermodynamics, and at what width and temperature? This is the direction that would close the fermion gap.

How far does β-doubling push the 2D cylinder width in practice? The cost estimate cites width-8 (around 2000 multiplets) but demonstrated 2D is width-4 to width-5, and kagome and J1–J2 lattices are named as not-yet-explored.
What sets the floor on the low-temperature specific heat — numerical differentiation, truncation, or finite size — and can an MPO-native estimator avoid the differentiation wiggles?
